---
title: "Same Bread, Five Verdicts"
subtitle: "Part 1 of a series on the meta-NPS. Five expert systems score one loaf of whole-wheat bread five different ways. Here is how we turn that disagreement into a measurement, and why it matters for policy."
author:
  - "Josh Erndt-Marino, PhD"
  - "FRESH's AI assistant"
date: "2026-06-23"
description: |
  Five expert-built food-rating systems score the same whole-wheat bread anywhere
  from the 12th to the 99th percentile of the US food supply. This first part of
  the meta-NPS series walks through what a rating system is, why the five disagree,
  and how a meta-rating turns that disagreement into a number you can act on.
categories: [meta-NPS, NPS, IAFNS, FRESH, interactive, series]
resources:
  - boxplot.html
  - processing.html
  - density.html
---

The prevailing story about nutrition goes something like this. We basically know
what is healthy. The hard parts are downstream: getting people to act on it (the
behavioral environment), and building places where the healthy choice is
available and affordable (the physical, or food, environment). On this account
the science of *what* is largely settled, and the real work is delivery.

It is a reasonable story, and it is mostly right at the level of slogans. Eat
more vegetables. Cut back on added sugar. The trouble starts the moment you have
to be specific, because labels, "healthy" claims, school and hospital
procurement, reformulation targets, and diet apps all run on the specific
version, not the slogan. They need a verdict on *this* food.

Here is one loaf of whole-wheat bread that shows how quickly the specific version
falls apart.

## What a rating system actually is

Most people have never heard of a nutrient profiling system, or NPS, even though
their food choices are increasingly shaped by them. The short version: take
something like a Nutrition Facts panel, run it through a set of expert-chosen
rules about what to reward and what to penalize, and collapse the result into a
single score. That is the entire move.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 240" role="img" aria-label="A Nutrition Facts panel run through expert-chosen rules produces a single score" style="width:100%;height:auto;display:block;margin:1.2rem 0;font-family:'Source Sans 3',Segoe UI,sans-serif">
  <!-- panel -->
  <rect x="14" y="30" width="180" height="180" rx="8" fill="#fff" stroke="#1a3a2a" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="104" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#1a3a2a">Nutrition Facts</text>
  <line x1="30" y1="66" x2="178" y2="66" stroke="#1a3a2a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <g font-size="11" fill="#43534a">
    <text x="30" y="88">Calories</text><rect x="120" y="80" width="50" height="8" rx="2" fill="#c8a24a"/>
    <text x="30" y="110">Total fat</text><rect x="120" y="102" width="30" height="8" rx="2" fill="#9aaa9a"/>
    <text x="30" y="132">Sodium</text><rect x="120" y="124" width="42" height="8" rx="2" fill="#9aaa9a"/>
    <text x="30" y="154">Fiber</text><rect x="120" y="146" width="22" height="8" rx="2" fill="#3f7d52"/>
    <text x="30" y="176">Sugars</text><rect x="120" y="168" width="38" height="8" rx="2" fill="#9aaa9a"/>
    <text x="30" y="198">Protein</text><rect x="120" y="190" width="28" height="8" rx="2" fill="#3f7d52"/>
  </g>
  <!-- arrow -->
  <path d="M205 120 h44" stroke="#6b7b6b" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <!-- rules -->
  <rect x="262" y="44" width="190" height="152" rx="10" fill="#fbfaf6" stroke="#2a5a3a" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="357" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="#1a3a2a">Expert-chosen rules</text>
  <g font-size="11.5" fill="#43534a">
    <text x="280" y="98">+ reward fiber, protein,</text>
    <text x="288" y="114">nutrients, food groups</text>
    <text x="280" y="140">− penalize sugar, sodium,</text>
    <text x="288" y="156">saturated fat, processing</text>
    <text x="280" y="182" font-style="italic" fill="#6b7b6b">…each with a chosen weight</text>
  </g>
  <!-- arrow -->
  <path d="M462 120 h44" stroke="#6b7b6b" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <!-- score -->
  <circle cx="592" cy="120" r="74" fill="#fff" stroke="#c8a24a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="592" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#6b7b6b">one</text>
  <text x="592" y="134" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" font-weight="800" fill="#1a3a2a">score</text>
  <text x="592" y="156" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#6b7b6b">0 – 100</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="ah" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7" refY="4.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L9,4.5 L0,9 z" fill="#6b7b6b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

An NPS is a formalized, transparent, reproducible expression of one expert
group's definition of "healthy." Change the rules, change the weights, or change
which features you look at in the first place, and you change the score. There is
no underlying true value the rules are estimating badly. Each set of rules *is* a
definition.

## Five definitions, one bread

Researchers have published more than 200 such systems, plus a long commercial
tail. For this series we use five widely cited ones. They were built by serious
people, and none is wrong on its own terms. They simply encode different
definitions.

```{=html}
<div class="sys-grid">
  <a class="sys-card" href="https://doi.org/10.3389/fnut.2020.00107" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <div class="sys-head">
      <span class="sys-ico" aria-hidden="true">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 48 48"><circle cx="24" cy="24" r="16" fill="#fff" stroke="#2a5a3a" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="19" cy="20" r="4.2" fill="#3f7d52"/><circle cx="29.5" cy="19.5" r="4.2" fill="#c8a24a"/><circle cx="24" cy="29.5" r="4.2" fill="#8fb89a"/></svg>
      </span>
      <div><span class="sys-tag">Nutrient density + food groups</span><h3>NRF 4:3:3</h3></div>
    </div>
    <div class="sys-paper">
      <span class="sys-jrnl">Frontiers in Nutrition · 2020</span>
      <span class="sys-ttl">New Nutrient Rich Food Nutrient Density Models That Include Nutrients and MyPlate Food Groups</span>
      <span class="sys-auth">Drewnowski &amp; Fulgoni</span>
    </div>
    <p>Adds up beneficial nutrients and MyPlate food groups, subtracts the nutrients to limit. Rewards nutrient-dense whole foods. Says nothing about processing.</p>
    <span class="sys-link">Read the paper →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="sys-card" href="https://doi.org/10.3945/jn.108.101360" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <div class="sys-head">
      <span class="sys-ico" aria-hidden="true">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 48 48"><line x1="24" y1="11" x2="24" y2="35" stroke="#2a5a3a" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="13" y1="16" x2="35" y2="16" stroke="#2a5a3a" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="24" cy="10" r="2.4" fill="#2a5a3a"/><path d="M9 16 a4 4 0 0 0 8 0" fill="none" stroke="#3f7d52" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M31 16 a4 4 0 0 0 8 0" fill="none" stroke="#b4532f" stroke-width="2.5"/><rect x="18" y="35" width="12" height="3" rx="1.5" fill="#2a5a3a"/></svg>
      </span>
      <div><span class="sys-tag">Nutrient density</span><h3>NRF 9.3</h3></div>
    </div>
    <div class="sys-paper">
      <span class="sys-jrnl">Journal of Nutrition · 2009</span>
      <span class="sys-ttl">Development and Validation of the Nutrient-Rich Foods Index: A Tool to Measure Nutritional Quality of Foods</span>
      <span class="sys-auth">Fulgoni, Keast &amp; Drewnowski</span>
    </div>
    <p>Nine nutrients to encourage minus three to limit, per 100 kcal. The classic nutrient-density score. Also blind to processing.</p>
    <span class="sys-link">Read the paper →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="sys-card" href="https://doi.org/10.1024/0300-9831/a000722" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <div class="sys-head">
      <span class="sys-ico" aria-hidden="true">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 48 48"><rect x="7" y="30" width="6.4" height="8" rx="1" fill="#3f7d52"/><rect x="15" y="26" width="6.4" height="12" rx="1" fill="#7fae5a"/><rect x="23" y="22" width="6.4" height="16" rx="1" fill="#e7c14a"/><rect x="31" y="17" width="6.4" height="21" rx="1" fill="#e0863f"/><rect x="39" y="12" width="6.4" height="26" rx="1" fill="#b4532f"/></svg>
      </span>
      <div><span class="sys-tag">Front-of-pack grade</span><h3>Nutri-Score</h3></div>
    </div>
    <div class="sys-paper">
      <span class="sys-jrnl">Int. J. Vitamin &amp; Nutrition Research · 2022</span>
      <span class="sys-ttl">The Nutri-Score nutrition label: a public health tool based on rigorous scientific evidence</span>
      <span class="sys-auth">Julia &amp; Hercberg · adapted from the British FSA/Ofcom model</span>
    </div>
    <p>Balances energy, sugar, saturated fat and salt against fiber, protein and fruit/veg into an A–E color grade. In use across Europe.</p>
    <span class="sys-link">Read the paper →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="sys-card proc" href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s43016-021-00381-y" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <div class="sys-head">
      <span class="sys-ico proc" aria-hidden="true">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 48 48"><circle cx="24" cy="24" r="16" fill="#fff" stroke="#7a6cae" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M24 11 L28 24 L24 24 Z" fill="#b4532f"/><path d="M24 11 L20 24 L24 24 Z" fill="#d96b4a"/><path d="M24 37 L20 24 L24 24 Z" fill="#7a6cae"/><path d="M24 37 L28 24 L24 24 Z" fill="#9a8fce"/><circle cx="24" cy="24" r="2.3" fill="#1a3a2a"/></svg>
      </span>
      <div><span class="sys-tag proc">Composite · weighs processing</span><h3>Food Compass</h3></div>
    </div>
    <div class="sys-paper">
      <span class="sys-jrnl">Nature Food · 2021</span>
      <span class="sys-ttl">Food Compass is a nutrient profiling system using expanded characteristics for assessing healthfulness of foods</span>
      <span class="sys-auth">Mozaffarian, El-Abbadi, O’Hearn et al.</span>
    </div>
    <p>Scores nine domains at once, including additives and degree of processing, on a 1–100 scale. Broad in scope, and openly contested.</p>
    <span class="sys-link">Read the paper →</span>
  </a>
  <a class="sys-card proc" href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-37457-1" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <div class="sys-head">
      <span class="sys-ico proc" aria-hidden="true">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 48 48"><path d="M10 38 V24 L19 28 V24 L28 28 V24 L37 28 V38 Z" fill="#fff" stroke="#7a6cae" stroke-width="2.2" stroke-linejoin="round"/><rect x="31" y="13" width="4" height="12" fill="#7a6cae"/><circle cx="33" cy="10" r="2" fill="#9a8fce"/><circle cx="36" cy="7.5" r="1.4" fill="#bdb3dc"/><rect x="14" y="31" width="4" height="4" fill="#9a8fce"/><rect x="22" y="31" width="4" height="4" fill="#9a8fce"/><rect x="30" y="31" width="4" height="4" fill="#9a8fce"/></svg>
      </span>
      <div><span class="sys-tag proc">Processing only</span><h3>FPro</h3></div>
    </div>
    <div class="sys-paper">
      <span class="sys-jrnl">Nature Communications · 2023</span>
      <span class="sys-ttl">Machine learning prediction of the degree of food processing</span>
      <span class="sys-auth">Menichetti, Ravandi, Mozaffarian &amp; Barabási</span>
    </div>
    <p>A machine-learning estimate of how processed a food is, from whole to ultra-processed. Ignores nutrients entirely.</p>
    <span class="sys-link">Read the paper →</span>
  </a>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
These five are a sample. With 200-plus systems in the literature, any comparison
sees a fraction of the field. Each system is also a living, partial definition:
it captures some of what people mean by "healthy" and leaves the rest out. Keep
that in mind when the numbers below start to look large. They are a floor.
:::

## Same bread, five verdicts

So: one loaf, five definitions. Watch what happens when each one scores it. The
walk-through builds itself; let it run, or step through with the arrows.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="boxFrame" src="boxplot.html?v=12" title="Interactive: five systems scoring whole-wheat bread" style="height:500px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

Every score here is a percentile: a food's rank against roughly 6,450 US foods.
That percentile is just the common ruler that lets five very different systems be
compared on one axis. The number to watch is the **score**.

The middle of the five scores is the **meta-score**, a single stand-in for the
loaf. How tightly the five agree is its **stability**. The **FRI** (Food
Recommendation Index) folds the two together: a food with a respectable middle
can still be marked down when the experts split on it, and whole-wheat bread is
exactly that case. Its scores run from 12 to 99, so the meta-rating does not call
it "so-so." It calls it **Uncertain**, one of four verdicts the method can
return: Certainly Healthy, Certainly Neutral, Certainly Unhealthy, or Uncertain.
"Uncertain" is not a hedge. It is a measured statement that the experts genuinely
do not agree.

## The fight was about processing

The disagreement on bread is not noise. It has a source, and the meta-rating can
point straight at it.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="procFrame" src="processing.html?v=12" title="Interactive: removing the two processing-aware systems" style="height:600px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

Two of the five systems weigh one extra thing the others ignore: how processed
the food is. Set those two aside, and the remaining three nearly agree (69, 93,
99). The meta-score climbs from 74 to 93, the spread collapses, and the verdict
flips from Uncertain to Certainly Healthy. Same loaf, opposite reading. The
entire fight was about processing.

That does not settle whether processing *should* count. It does something more
useful: it locates the disagreement precisely, and tells NPS designers exactly
what they would need to agree on (or transparently disagree about) to stop
producing this particular split. Treating the disagreement as information, rather
than choosing a winner among the systems, is the whole idea of a meta-rating.

## Five systems, five food supplies

Zoom out from one loaf to the whole supply, and the disagreement gets harder to
wave away. Each system draws its own picture of how healthy American food is.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="densFrame" src="density.html?v=10" title="Interactive: five systems, five food-supply distributions" style="height:540px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

The food supply does not have one healthiness shape. It has five, depending on
which expert you trust. Run the meta-rating across all of them and the contested
zone is not a rounding error: about one in five carbohydrate-rich foods, and
roughly half of all grains, fall into "Uncertain," where the experts cannot agree.

Those numbers are a floor, not a ceiling, and conservatively so on four counts:

1. We sampled only **5 of 200-plus** published systems. More systems, more disagreement.
2. We used **one** way of drawing the cutoffs (spread and rank-range). A different defensible rule flags more foods.
3. We held each system to **one** faithful implementation, when a single system can contradict itself on something as basic as how to normalize its inputs. The disagreement also lives *inside* one expert.
4. Every system here is a **universal** rater: one score per food, the same for everyone. None account for the eater (physiology, goals, context). Healthfulness that depends on the person is disagreement these numbers never even attempt to measure.

Even under all four conservative choices, the disagreement is this large.

## Where the leverage actually is

Step back to the prevailing story we opened with. We mostly know what is healthy;
the work is behavior and access. Bread suggests a third environment the story
leaves out: the **informational** one. Do we even agree on, and clearly
communicate, what "healthy" is? On the high-stakes, food-by-food questions that
policy actually runs on, we frequently do not, and we have no shared way to show
the public how settled or contested any given verdict is.

That third environment is the neglected, lower-hanging, higher-leverage lever.
Harmonizing definitions and communicating uncertainty is faster and cheaper than
rebuilding food access or shifting population behavior, and it feeds both of the
others. Clearer definitions make for better labels, procurement, and
reformulation (the physical environment). Honest, uncertainty-aware information
starves the misinformation vacuum and gives behavior something solid to stand on.

Two honest limits keep this from becoming a slogan of its own. Information
enables; it does not cure. Healthy foods are not the same as healthy diets, and
no rating system fixes the gap between the two. And harmonizing the universal
disagreement is only the first move. The second is personalizing beyond it, since
"healthy for whom" is a question none of these systems ask. Harmonization is the
floor; personalization is the ceiling. Both are informational-environment work,
and both are buildable now.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Whole-wheat bread's five scores are real, drawn from the published systems run
across the US food supply. This is the first of a three-part series. Part 2 takes
the meta-rating across the whole supply to ask where the disagreements cluster.
Part 3 brings in consumers, and what happens when expert verdicts and public
belief pull apart. The webinars and papers behind all of it are on the
[resources page](../resources/).
:::
```{=html}
<script>
(function(){
  var frames=document.querySelectorAll("iframe.story-frame");
  function fit(f){ try{ var d=f.contentDocument; if(d&&d.body){ f.style.height=(d.body.scrollHeight+8)+"px"; } }catch(e){} }
  function fitAll(){ frames.forEach(fit); }
  frames.forEach(function(f){
    f.addEventListener("load",function(){ fit(f); setTimeout(function(){fit(f);},400); });
    if("IntersectionObserver" in window){
      var io=new IntersectionObserver(function(es){
        es.forEach(function(en){ if(en.isIntersecting){ try{ f.contentWindow.postMessage("play-story","*"); }catch(e){} } });
      }, {threshold:0.25});
      io.observe(f);
    }
  });
  // a chart re-fits its own height on reflow, then asks us to resize its iframe
  window.addEventListener("message",function(e){ if(e&&e.data==="story-fit") fitAll(); });
  window.addEventListener("resize",function(){ fitAll(); setTimeout(fitAll,160); });
})();
</script>
```
